story:
On a freezing day in the city’s hollowed-out lower downtown, Russell muttered, “I don’t belong here,” and pulled his tattered overcoat tight against the wind that chased trash along empty streets. What’s that? You want to know about Russell? Average height, unremarkable, hair too long and too dirty, beard like sandpaper. Skin weathered into creases you could fall into, eyes so dark they might be all pupil, the kind that see straight through you. Unemployed and, as people say when they’re being polite, unhoused.

He moved past boarded factories and apartments that leaned like tired old men. Debris scuttled ahead of him as if trying to escape. Then he stopped at a dead neon sign above a chained door: Breakfast Served All Day. Pressing his palm to the glass, he saw himself younger—cleaner, tie crooked, laugh easy—sitting with his crew from the plant while coffee flowed and eggs piled high. Back then, it felt like the noise and grease and jokes would never end.

But the plant “paused,” then died. Promises were made, help was pledged, and nothing came. When the last machines left, so did Russell’s paycheck, then his pride, then his home. “I don’t belong here either,” he whispered to the diner that had outlasted the life it once contained.

A cough snapped the quiet. From the shadow of a boarded pharmacy, a boy stepped out—thin as a fence post, jacket too small, hands raw, a backpack clutched like a lifeline. “You lost, kid?” Russell asked. “No more lost than you,” the boy said, voice cracked by cold or fear. The two of them fell into step, the boy’s shoes squeaking over ice, Russell’s cough rattling his ribs.

They passed a mural, faded blues and yellows peeling from brick. Russell remembered when it was new, a “revitalization” project, and his daughter’s hand tugging at his own. “Look, Daddy! It’s the sun!” He had lifted her to his shoulders and, for a breath, felt like the father he wanted to be. Now the paint was grimy, his daughter grown and gone, and the street full of ghosts. “What’s wrong?” the boy asked. “Ghosts,” Russell said.

At the end of the block, stained-glass windows leaked warm light. A hand-painted sign: Soup Kitchen – Open Tonight. Steam, a line, the sounds of people who had run out of elsewhere. Russell stopped, torn between warmth and the shame that always waited inside. “We could go in,” the boy said. “Not for me,” Russell answered, and his stomach betrayed him with a growl. The boy didn’t smile. “Then it is for you.”

“Alright, kid,” Russell sighed. “So you don’t keel over on the sidewalk.”

Inside, stew and coffee filled the air, and the sudden heat stung his skin. A woman in a knit sweater placed a steaming cup in Russell’s hands without asking anything of him. Instinct told him to leave. The boy looked back and motioned him over. Russell lifted the cup. Bitter, alive heat slid down his throat. He took a step forward.

He still didn’t know where he belonged. But maybe belonging wasn’t a place. Maybe it was a choice. Tonight, he chose not to walk away.

And the boy—was he just a boy, or something more, guiding Russell to a door he couldn’t open alone? That story might have to wait.
